title: Bipolar Disorder and Mania
subtitle: The nurse cares for a manic patient — keeping them safe, lowering stimulation, setting firm limits, offering portable high-calorie foods, and monitoring lithium levels and toxicity.

patient.name: Bipolar Disorder / Mania
patient.admitDate: The nurse safely supports a patient in a manic episode.

nursesNote: The nurse cares for a patient with bipolar disorder, a chronic but treatable mood disorder with swings between mania and depression. In a manic episode the patient shows an elevated or irritable mood, a decreased need for sleep, grandiosity, rapid or pressured speech, racing ideas, distractibility, and impulsive, risky behavior such as overspending or hypersexuality with poor judgment. Safety is the priority: the nurse sets firm, consistent limits, reduces environmental stimulation, provides structure, and protects the patient from harmful impulses. Because a manic patient is often too busy and distracted to sit and eat, the nurse offers portable, high-calorie, high-protein finger foods and fluids to prevent weight loss and dehydration, and promotes rest. Lithium is a common mood stabilizer with a narrow therapeutic range (about 0.6 to 1.2 mEq/L); the nurse monitors blood levels and watches for toxicity (nausea, vomiting, diarrhea, fine then coarse tremor, confusion, and ataxia), holding the dose and notifying the provider if toxicity is suspected. The patient is taught to keep sodium and fluid intake steady, since low sodium or dehydration raises the lithium level, and to avoid abrupt sodium or fluid changes. Lithium takes one to two weeks to work, so an antipsychotic or benzodiazepine may be used to manage acute mania in the meantime. The nurse supports adherence and follow-up.
providerOrders: Maintain safety and set firm, consistent limits
Provide a calm, low-stimulation, structured environment
Offer portable high-calorie finger foods and fluids
Monitor lithium levels and assess for toxicity
Teach steady sodium and fluid intake and medication adherence

opts.text: Mania includes a decreased need for sleep
Grandiosity and rapid speech are common
Impulsive, risky behavior can occur
Manic patients reliably sit and eat full meals
Safety and limit-setting are priorities
Bipolar disorder swings between mania and depression
A low-stimulation, structured environment helps
Portable high-calorie finger foods support nutrition
Lithium has a wide, forgiving therapeutic range
Low sodium or dehydration raises lithium levels
Tremor, vomiting, and confusion suggest lithium toxicity
Lithium works immediately for acute mania

opts.text: Ensure safety and reduce stimulation
Set clear, consistent limits
Offer portable high-calorie foods and fluids
Give medications and monitor lithium level
Promote rest and document response

steps.title: Take Action
steps.sub: A patient on lithium has vomiting, diarrhea, coarse tremor, and confusion
steps.inst: Rank the order of actions. #1 = FIRST.

opts.text: Hold the next lithium dose
Assess the patient and recent intake
Draw a lithium level as ordered
Notify the provider of suspected toxicity
Monitor and document fluids and status
Offering a sandwich the patient can carry and eat
Placing the manic patient in a busy dayroom
Keeping sodium and fluid intake steady on lithium
Stopping lithium because the patient feels fine
Setting firm, consistent limits on behavior
Ignoring tremor and confusion on lithium
Monitoring lithium blood levels regularly
Arguing and matching the patient's intensity
